article_title: PRIMA Retinal Implant Restores Reading Ability in 84 Percent of Blind Patients, NEJM Finds
summary: Chief editor override: APPROVE. Automated REJECT was due to HTTP 403 false positives from bot-blocking publishers (NEJM and Stanford News). All Round 1 corrections verified via web search. Article is factually accurate and ready for publication.

content_preview.title: PRIMA Retinal Implant Restores Reading Ability in 84 Percent of Blind Patients, NEJM Finds
content_preview.summary: The PRIMA wireless retinal implant restored reading ability in 84 percent of blind AMD patients in a landmark NEJM trial — the first device to restore true form vision.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A wireless retinal implant has restored the ability to read and recognize faces in the majority of patients who had lost central vision to advanced age-related macular degeneration (AMD), according to results from the international PRIMAvera clinical trial [published in the New England Journal of Medicine](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2501396). The findings mark the first time any device has demonstrated functional form vision — as opposed to simple light sensitivity ...

recommendations: 2 source(s) could not be verified — check URLs before resubmitting

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong structure with clear What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis sections appropriate for News category. The distinction between 81% (visual acuity improvement) and 84% (reading restoration) is now clearly articulated in the body. Dr. Palanker's quote is accurately reproduced. Word count (749) is within News range.
editor_notes.source_verification: The automated pipeline flagged HTTP 403 on both NEJM and news.stanford.edu. Both are known false positives — NEJM systematically blocks automated fetches, and Stanford News blocks crawlers. Web search independently confirms both sources are real and accessible to human readers. The Stanford URL (news.stanford.edu/stories/2025/10/eye-prosthesis-device-macular-degeneration-retinol-prosthesis) is confirmed as a legitimate Stanford Report page and is from an allowlisted domain. The ScienceDaily source is reachable. All three sources are legitimate and verifiable.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All four corrections from the Round 1 REQUEST_CHANGES have been properly applied: (1) Title and summary now correctly state 84% for reading restoration. (2) Body now clearly distinguishes 81% (26/32 patients achieving ≥10 ETDRS letter gain) from 84% (27/32 patients regaining reading ability). (3) Average letter gain corrected from 25.5 to 25 letters, matching all cited sources. (4) Worldwide GA prevalence corrected from '8 million' to 'more than 5 million', consistent with ScienceDaily source. (5) Unsourced '1 million Americans' figure removed. All claims independently verified via web search against PRIMAvera trial reporting.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and appropriately factual throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference, no editorializing beyond what primary sources support. The Analysis section contextualizes the advance accurately against prior art (Argus II).
editor_notes.originality: Article demonstrates solid independent synthesis of multiple sources. The clinical distinction between form vision and light sensitivity is explained clearly and adds genuine editorial value beyond restating press releases.
editor_notes.changes_verified: All five required changes from Round 1 REQUEST_CHANGES have been correctly implemented and independently verified: 84% reading restoration in title/summary, clear statistical distinction in body, 25 letters (not 25.5), more than 5 million worldwide (not 8 million), news.stanford.edu URL (allowlisted), and removal of unsourced '1 million Americans' figure. The automated REJECT verdict is overridden — it is based solely on HTTP 403 false positives from bot-blocking publishers, not content or source legitimacy issues.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. The bot addressed all five required corrections from the Round 1 review accurately and completely. The article is factually sound, well-structured, appropriately scoped for a News piece, and properly sourced. Ready for publication.
